title: Baseline First
concept: A baseline is a simple starting system that gives you a reference point, so you can tell whether a new deep-learning idea is actually progress or just complexity without evidence.

exercises.prompt: Why should teams build a simple baseline before a fancy model?

options.text: Because without a reference point, “better” is hard to prove.
Because deep learning should always avoid measurement.
Because simple systems can never be useful.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A baseline tells you what counts as real improvement.

cards.title: Start with a road sign, not a treasure hunt
cards.body: A team spends two weeks on a complex transformer for text classification. It reaches 88% accuracy. Later they try a simple bag-of-words baseline and discover it reaches 87%. Suddenly the fancy model looks less like a breakthrough and more like a small tradeoff.

cards.title: What a baseline does
cards.body: A baseline is a simple, reasonable starting solution used as a reference. It does not need to be glamorous. It needs to be clear, measurable, and hard to fool. Then every new model has to earn its complexity by beating that anchor.

cards.title: Beat the warm-up score before claiming greatness
cards.body: If you play a basketball shooting game, the first thing you want is a score to beat. Without that reference, every later round feels impressive in isolation. Baselines give machine-learning projects that same scoreboard.

cards.title: A simple spam filter can teach a lot
cards.body: For spam detection, a baseline might be logistic regression on word counts. If a giant neural model only improves a tiny amount while costing much more to train and serve, the team learns that the fancy solution may not be worth it yet.

cards.title: Simple does not mean embarrassing
cards.body: A common myth is that baselines are just beginner toys. In practice, strong teams love baselines because they expose weak ideas early. A baseline can save weeks by revealing that a supposed innovation barely improves on a much simpler method.

left.text:
A result may sound impressive.
Hard to judge whether complexity paid off.
Teams can fool themselves easily.

right.text:
Simple reference is measured early.
New models must beat it clearly.
Progress becomes easier to trust.

exercises.prompt: Which system best fits the idea of a baseline?

options.text: A simple, reasonable model used as a reference point.
The most complicated model the team can imagine.
A result with no evaluation metrics.
A hidden model no one is allowed to compare against.

exercises.explain: Right. A baseline is useful because it gives the team something concrete to beat.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a simple baseline is strong, that is valuable information, not a failure.
exercises.explain: True. A strong baseline protects the team from adding complexity without enough payoff.

exercises.sentence: A baseline gives later experiments a clear point of ___.
exercises.explain: Comparison is correct. That anchor is what makes later gains interpretable.

exercises.prompt: Which team is using the baseline-first mindset well?

options.text: They train a simple reference model before testing a more advanced architecture.
They call the first fancy result a breakthrough without any reference point.
They skip simple models because only complexity counts as real work.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A baseline is not the final answer, but it is the first honest checkpoint.

exercises.prompt: A startup claims its new deep model is amazing, but it never compared the model to a simple baseline. Why should you be cautious?

options.text: Because without a baseline, it is unclear whether the extra complexity delivered meaningful progress.
Because baselines guarantee that advanced models can never win.
Because simple methods are the only valid tools in machine learning.
Because benchmarks should be avoided whenever possible.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A strong claim needs an anchor, and the baseline provides that anchor.
